meta.name: ASI-3 Anxiety Sensitivity Index
meta.description: The ASI-3 is an 18-item self-report scale measuring anxiety sensitivity, which is the fear of anxiety-related sensations based on beliefs that these sensations have harmful consequences. It assesses three dimensions: physical, cognitive, and social concerns.
meta.author: Taylor et al. (2007)
meta.reference: Taylor S, Zvolensky MJ, Cox BJ, Deacon B, Heimberg RG, Ledley DR, Abramowitz JS, Holaway RM, Sandin B, Stewart SH, Coles M, Eng W, Daly ES, Arrindell WA, Bouvard M, Cardenas SJ. Robust dimensions of anxiety sensitivity: development and initial validation of the Anxiety Sensitivity Index-3. Psychol Assess. 2007;19(2):176-188.
meta.instruction: Please indicate the degree to which you agree with each item by selecting the most appropriate option.

questions.text: It is important for me not to appear nervous
When I cannot keep my mind on a task, I worry that I might be going crazy
It scares me when my heart beats rapidly
When my stomach is upset, I worry that I might be seriously ill
It scares me when I am unable to keep my mind on a task
When I tremble in the presence of others, I fear what people might think of me
When my chest feels tight, I get scared that I will not be able to breathe properly
When I feel pain in my chest, I worry that I am going to have a heart attack
I worry that other people will notice my anxiety
When I feel spacey or spaced out I worry that I may be mentally ill
It scares me when I blush in front of people
When I notice my heart skipping a beat, I worry that there is something seriously wrong with me
When I begin to sweat in a social situation, I fear people will think negatively of me
When my thoughts seem to speed up, I worry that I might be going crazy
When my throat feels tight, I worry that I could choke to death
When I have trouble thinking clearly, I worry that there is something wrong with me
I think it would be horrible for me to faint in public
When my mind goes blank, I worry there is something terribly wrong with me

ranges.label: Low Anxiety Sensitivity
ranges.description: Your overall anxiety sensitivity is low. You generally do not interpret normal bodily sensations as threatening or dangerous. This balanced perspective on physical and mental experiences is a protective factor.
suggestions: Continue maintaining your balanced and adaptive perspective on bodily sensations and mental experiences
Your low anxiety sensitivity serves as a protective buffer against developing panic and anxiety disorders
Stay informed about anxiety symptoms so you can recognize changes early if they occur

ranges.label: Moderate Anxiety Sensitivity
ranges.description: You have a moderate level of anxiety sensitivity. Certain anxiety-related sensations such as rapid heartbeat, racing thoughts, or visible nervousness may cause you notable concern or discomfort at times.
suggestions: Educate yourself about the nature of anxiety and how physical symptoms are normal stress responses
Practice relaxation techniques such as diaphragmatic breathing and progressive muscle relaxation
Challenge catastrophic interpretations of bodily sensations by examining evidence for and against them
Keep a journal to track which sensations trigger worry and identify patterns in your anxiety responses
Consider consulting a therapist if anxiety sensitivity is beginning to limit your daily activities

ranges.label: High Anxiety Sensitivity
ranges.description: Your anxiety sensitivity is high. You may frequently misinterpret normal bodily sensations as indicators of serious illness, mental breakdown, or social embarrassment, fueling a cycle of escalating anxiety.
suggestions: Consulting a mental health professional experienced in anxiety disorders is strongly recommended
Interoceptive exposure therapy involving safe, gradual exposure to feared sensations is highly effective
Cognitive restructuring can help you develop more realistic interpretations of bodily sensations
Practice grounding techniques when physical sensations trigger anxious thoughts

ranges.label: Very High Anxiety Sensitivity
ranges.description: Your anxiety sensitivity is very high. This extreme level is strongly associated with panic disorder, generalized anxiety, and avoidance behaviors that can severely restrict daily life and functioning.
suggestions: Seeking professional help from a psychologist or psychiatrist is strongly recommended at this level
Evidence-based treatments such as cognitive behavioral therapy have strong efficacy for anxiety sensitivity
Discuss both therapy and potential medication options with a qualified mental health professional
Gradually and safely confronting feared sensations under professional guidance accelerates recovery

physical.label: Low
physical.description: Low concern about physical anxiety sensations.

physical.label: Moderate
physical.description: Moderate concern about physical anxiety sensations.

physical.label: High
physical.description: High concern about physical anxiety sensations.

physical.label: Very High
physical.description: Very high concern about physical anxiety sensations.

cognitive.label: Low
cognitive.description: Low concern about cognitive anxiety symptoms.

cognitive.label: Moderate
cognitive.description: Moderate concern about cognitive anxiety symptoms.

cognitive.label: High
cognitive.description: High concern about cognitive anxiety symptoms.

cognitive.label: Very High
cognitive.description: Very high concern about cognitive anxiety symptoms.

social.label: Low
social.description: Low concern about social consequences of anxiety.

social.label: Moderate
social.description: Moderate concern about social consequences of anxiety.

social.label: High
social.description: High concern about social consequences of anxiety.

social.label: Very High
social.description: Very high concern about social consequences of anxiety.
